title: Charmed Life — 1 (Fragment 2)
sourceText:
“And where would you little people like to live?” he asked kindly.

Gwendolen at once said that old Mrs. Sharp downstairs had offered to take them in. “She’s been ever so kind to us,” she explained. “We’d love to live with her.”

Mrs. Sharp had been very kind. She was a witch too—the printed sign in her parlor window said Certified Witch —and interested in Gwendolen. The Mayor was a little dubious. Like all people who had no talent for witchcraft, he did not approve of those who had. He asked Cat how he felt about Gwendolen’s plan. Cat did not mind. He preferred living in the house he was used to, even if it was downstairs. Since the Mayor felt that the two orphans ought to be made as happy as possible, he agreed. Gwendolen and Cat moved in with Mrs. Sharp.

Looking back on it, Cat supposed that it was from this time on that he was certain Gwendolen was a witch. He had not been sure before. When he had asked his parents, they had shaken their heads, sighed, and looked unhappy. Cat had been puzzled, because he remembered the terrible trouble there had been when Gwendolen gave him cramps. He could not see how his parents could blame Gwendolen for it unless she truly was a witch. But all that was changed now. Mrs. Sharp made no secret of it.

“You’ve a real talent for magic, dearie,” she said, beaming at Gwendolen, “and I wouldn’t be doing my duty by you if I let it go to waste. We must see about a teacher for you right away. You could do worse than go to Mr. Nostrum next door for a start. He may be the worst necromancer in town, but he knows how to teach. He’ll give you a good grounding, my love.”

Mr. Nostrum’s charges for teaching magic turned out to be £1 an hour for the Elementary Grades, and a guinea an hour for the Advanced Grades beyond. Rather expensive, as Mrs. Sharp said. She put on her best hat with black beads and ran around to the Town Hall to see if the Fund would pay for Gwendolen’s lessons.

To her annoyance, the Mayor refused. He told Mrs. Sharp that witchcraft was not part of an ordinary education. Mrs. Sharp came back rattling the beads on her hat with irritation, and carrying a flat cardboard box the Mayor had given her, full of the odds and ends the kind ladies had cleared out of Gwendolen’s parents’ bedroom.

“Blind prejudice!” Mrs. Sharp said, dumping the box on the kitchen table. “If a person has a gift, they have a right to have it developed—and so I told him! But don’t worry, dearie,” she said, seeing that Gwendolen was looking decidedly stormy. “There’s a way around everything. Mr. Nostrum would teach you for nothing, if we found the right thing to tempt him with. Let’s have a look in this box. Your poor ma and pa may have left something that might be just the thing.”

words.definition: In a kind and gentle manner.
words.example: He asked kindly if they needed help.
words.exampleText: And where would you little people like to live? he asked kindly.

words.definition: Hesitating or doubting; not to be relied upon.
words.example: She felt dubious about the plan's success.
words.exampleText: The Mayor was a little dubious about those who had talent for witchcraft.

words.definition: To officially agree to or accept as satisfactory.
words.example: The manager did not approve of the changes.
words.exampleText: He did not approve of those who had talent for witchcraft.

words.definition: Like (one thing or person) better than another or others.
words.example: I prefer tea to coffee.
words.exampleText: Cat did not mind. He preferred living in the house he was used to.

words.definition: A child whose parents are dead.
words.example: The charity helps orphans find new homes.
words.exampleText: Since the Mayor felt that the two orphans ought to be made as happy as possible.

words.definition: Known for sure; established beyond doubt.
words.example: She was certain about the decision.
words.exampleText: Cat supposed that from this time on he was certain Gwendolen was a witch.

words.definition: Unable to understand; perplexed.
words.example: He looked puzzled by the instructions.
words.exampleText: Cat had been puzzled because he remembered the trouble.

words.definition: Sudden, involuntary contractions of muscles causing pain.
words.example: She suffered leg cramps after running.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen gave him cramps, which caused trouble.

words.definition: Something kept hidden from others.
words.example: They shared a secret only they knew.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp made no secret of Gwendolen being a witch.

words.definition: A natural aptitude or skill.
words.example: She has a talent for painting.
words.exampleText: You've a real talent for magic, dearie.

words.definition: The power to influence events by supernatural means.
words.example: He learned magic tricks as a hobby.
words.exampleText: A real talent for magic.

words.definition: A moral or legal obligation; a responsibility.
words.example: She felt it was her duty to help.
words.exampleText: I wouldn't be doing my duty by you if I let it go to waste.

words.definition: The use of something carelessly, extravagantly, or to no purpose.
words.example: Don't let food go to waste.
words.exampleText: Letting talent go to waste.

words.definition: A person who practices necromancy; a wizard who communicates with the dead.
words.example: Legends spoke of an evil necromancer.
words.exampleText: He may be the worst necromancer in town.

words.definition: Basic training or fundamental knowledge.
words.example: She received a good grounding in mathematics.
words.exampleText: He'll give you a good grounding, my love.

words.definition: The prices asked for goods or services.
words.example: The charges for the service were high.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum's charges for teaching magic.

words.definition: Costing a lot of money.
words.example: This dress is too expensive for me.
words.exampleText: Rather expensive, as Mrs. Sharp said.

words.definition: A feeling of being bothered or irritated.
words.example: Her lateness was a constant annoyance.
words.exampleText: To her annoyance, the Mayor refused.

words.definition: To make a rapid succession of short, sharp knocking sounds.
words.example: The window rattled in the wind.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp came back rattling the beads.

words.definition: Preconceived opinion not based on reason or actual experience.
words.example: They fought against racial prejudice.
words.exampleText: Blind prejudice!

words.definition: A natural ability or talent.
words.example: He has a gift for music.
words.exampleText: If a person has a gift, they have the right to develop it.

words.definition: To grow or cause to grow; to improve or be improved over time.
words.example: She developed her writing skills.
words.exampleText: They have a right to have the gift developed.

words.definition: Characterized by strong emotions or angry behaviour.
words.example: A stormy argument ensued.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen was looking decidedly stormy.

words.definition: To entice or try to persuade someone to do something often unwise.
words.example: He was tempted by the offer of money.
words.exampleText: If we found the right thing to tempt him with.

words.definition: Situated or moving in a circle or vicinity.
words.example: They sat around the table.
words.exampleText: She ran around to the Town Hall.
